name: Lyle
short_description: Rockhound Lyle for colorful agates, jasper, and petrified wood along the scenic White Salmon River valley. Gentle terrain and easy access make this a rewarding spot for collectors of all skill levels.
description:
Lyle sits in the heart of the White Salmon River drainage in Klickitat County, a geologically rich area straddling the transition zone between the Cascade Range and the Columbia River Plateau. This location offers access to deposits of banded agate, jasper, and petrified wood that eroded from Miocene-age basalt flows and silica-rich volcanic deposits common throughout the region. The gentle slope and lower elevation make this site accessible year-round, though spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.

The geology here reflects millions of years of volcanic activity followed by erosion. Ancient lava flows and pyroclastic deposits created the silica-rich environment where these gemstones formed. Over time, weathering and river erosion have exposed surface materials and scattered specimens across accessible terrain. Collectors often find specimens in washes, along hillsides, and in worked areas where previous rockhounders have already demonstrated the productivity of the site.

What makes Lyle appealing is the combination of productive terrain, relatively easy access, and the variety of collectible material. Agates here often display attractive banding and color variation, while jasper specimens range from red and brown to mottled varieties. Petrified wood finds add another dimension to collections. The gentle slope and proximity to parking make this suitable for families and less experienced rockhounds.

Bring containers to organize finds by type as you collect. The area can be productive after seasonal water flow has washed material downhill. Search washes and eroded banks first, then expand to nearby hillsides. Local knowledge helps - talk with other collectors if you encounter them, as they can point out the most reliable producing zones.
history: The Lyle area lies within the Columbia River Basalt Province, where extensive Miocene volcanic flows (16-10 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of basalt with intercalated silica-rich ash and tuff beds. These volcanic deposits underwent silicification and weathering processes that produced the agate, jasper, and petrified wood found here today. The White Salmon River valley has historically been a productive rockhounding area for regional collectors seeking these colorful stones.
tips: Search washes and eroded streamside banks first, as gravity and water flow concentrate lighter specimens. Look for banded agate with distinct color layers - whites, grays, browns, and occasional reds are common here. Use a small pry bar and chisel to carefully extract specimens from harder matrix, but hand-collect surface finds first. Early morning visits often reveal freshly exposed material after nighttime water movement, increasing your chances of quality finds.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1194 feet with gentle slope terrain. The area is vegetated with typical mid-elevation scrub and grass. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting zones is typically short (under 0.5 mile). Footing is uneven with rocky outcrops and loose scree common, but not treacherous. Trails and washes are generally easy to navigate.
best_season: April - June, September - November. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and lower water levels in washes. Summer heat can be intense at this elevation, and winter precipitation makes road access less reliable.
written_directions: From White Salmon (nearest town with services, approximately 12 miles northwest), head south on WA-14 toward Lyle. The community of Lyle is marked along WA-14 in the White Salmon River drainage. Parking and access points are available near the town area along WA-14 and adjacent side roads. Look for informal pullouts and established collector access routes along the river valley. A standard passenger vehicle can access main viewing and collecting areas, though high-clearance vehicles are better for rough side roads.
road_conditions: WA-14 is well-maintained. Some side roads and pullouts may be rough or partially improved, suitable for passenger vehicles with modest clearance.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small parking areas available along WA-14 near Lyle. Capacity is limited. Arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal collection only on public access points. Check current land status before collecting - some areas may be private property. Respect posted boundaries. Pack out all trash. Do not dig or damage vegetation. Removal of specimens for personal use collection is generally permitted on open BLM or state lands in this area, but always verify access rights before collecting.
rules:
Personal collection only
No commercial collecting
Respect private property boundaries
Pack out all trash
No motorized equipment for excavation
Leave no trace principles apply
nearest_services: White Salmon, WA - approximately 12 miles northwest. Gas, groceries, and dining available. Last services before entering more remote valley areas.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Parking near WA-14 minimizes walking distance. Uneven rocky footing and loose scree require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to collecting zones. Overall terrain is beginner-friendly compared to mountain rockhounding sites.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Lyle, Washington?
faq.answer: The primary gems found at Lyle are banded agate, colorful jasper, petrified wood, and chalcedony. Agates here often display beautiful banding in whites, grays, browns, and occasional reds. Jasper specimens range from solid reds and browns to mottled patterns. Petrified wood finds add variety to collections. All are collectible on public access areas.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Lyle free or are there fees?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Lyle is free. There are no permit requirements or collection fees for personal use on public land. Simply park at available pullouts along WA-14 and begin collecting. Always verify you are on public or accessible land before removing specimens.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Lyle?
faq.answer: April through June and September through November offer the best collecting conditions. Spring and fall temperatures are comfortable, and lower water levels in washes expose fresh material. Summer heat can be intense, and winter weather may limit road access. Morning visits often reveal newly exposed specimens.

faq.question: Is Lyle rockhounding suitable for families and beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Lyle is excellent for families and beginners. The gentle slope terrain, easy access via WA-14, and short walking distances make it beginner-friendly. Children ages 6 and up can participate. Surface specimens are common, so you do not need expensive equipment. Bring containers, sturdy shoes, and sun protection.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound at Lyle?
faq.answer: For Lyle, bring a small pry bar and chisel for extracting specimens from harder matrix, a hand lens to inspect agate banding, containers for organizing finds, heavy-duty gloves, and sturdy hiking boots for the rocky terrain. A small backpack is useful for carrying specimens and water. Sunscreen and a hat are essential even in spring and fall.

faq.question: How far is Lyle from White Salmon and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: Lyle is approximately 12 miles southeast of White Salmon along WA-14. White Salmon has gas stations, grocery stores, restaurants, and lodging. This makes it an ideal base for a day trip or weekend rockhounding adventure. Plan to fill up with gas and supplies before heading to remote valley areas.

meta_description: Find banded agate, jasper, and petrified wood at Lyle, Washington. Free public rockhounding near White Salmon. Easy terrain perfect for families.
